If there is stress on one syllable in a word, that means you have to emphasize that syllable.
So, if stress is on the first syllable, you should say the first syllable louder than the other two.
If it's on the second, you do it on the second one.
If it's on the third, you read the third syllable louder than the first and second.

As I said, the English language has rhythm at word scale,
which is called word stress, and at sentence scale, which is called intonation.
Now, what is intonation?
Intonation is the melody of your spoken English.
It is the rises and falls in your tone of voice as you speak.
So, perhaps sometimes you come down, and sometimes you go up, and sometimes you come down and go up.
This is your intonation.
There are three main patterns of intonation in the English language.
One of them is falling intonation, which means as you get to the end of the sentence,
your tone of voice goes down. It decreases.
For example, look at this. I have to work.
I have to work. I have to work.
My tone of voice goes down. I don't say, I have to work. No, I say, I have to work.
I go down. I have to work. When do we use this falling intonation?
We use it when something is certain.
I have to work. It means I'm definitely going to work.
That is for sure. Or when something is finished and over.
For example, I couldn't find it.
Look at the sentence. I couldn't find it. I couldn't find it.
I couldn't find it. I couldn't find it.
It means I tried to find it.
I searched, but eventually I couldn't find it.
It's over. It's finished. So, for something which is certain, or something which is finished.
Another pattern of intonation is rising intonation.
That means your tone of voice goes higher at the end of the sentence.
Now, where do we use rising intonation?
In yes-no questions. For example, is he sad?
Listen. Is he sad? Is he sad?
Is she our doctor? Is she our doctor?
Is she our doctor? So, you see, your tone of voice goes higher at the end of the sentence.
Now, look at these two. She's our doctor. She's our doctor.
Falling intonation. Is she our doctor? Is she our doctor?
Rising intonation. Now, there is a third pattern of intonation, which is called the fall-rise.
It means, first, your tone of voice goes down, then it comes up again.
When do we use it? When our sentence is not complete yet,
and we want to continue. For example, I don't play tennis,
but I like watching it. Listen.
I don't play tennis, but I like watching it.
I don't play tennis. Tennis.

What are contractions or contracted forms?
Contractions are the shorter form of a longer sentence.
For example, the contracted form of going to is gonna.
I am going to travel. I'm gonna travel.
